title: P1‐388: ASSOCIATION BETWEEN POLYGENIC RISK SCORE AND INCREASED BRAIN TAU DEPOSITION IN COGNITIVELY NORMAL AND MCI INDIVIDUALS
abstract: study to show serum plasmalogen levels to be signiﬁcantly associated with neuroimaging AD biomarkers and genetic variation in LIPC and TLR2 . Both genes have been previously implicated in AD, LIPC is further associated with circulating lipoproteins affecting cardiovascular disease risk. Our results provide further evidence implicating altered plasmalogen dynamics in AD and suggest a role for these lipids in the linkage between vascular disease and AD. Background: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA) is characterized by cerebrovasculardepositionofamyloidbetawhichleadstothedevelop-mentofcerebralmicrobleeds(CMB).Theassociationbetweenmicro-bleeds and intracranial atherosclerosis is debated. We sought to evaluatetheassociationbetweenCMBandintracranialatherosclerosis(ICAS)inpatientswithCAA. Methods: A retrospective cohort of patients from tertiary hospital in South Korea, Asan Medical Center diagnosed with probable CAA per Boston criteria was examined. Brain magnetic resonance angiography imaging was rated for presence of > 50% intracranial stenosis. Using multivariable logis-tic regression, we related right and left middle cerebral artery (MCA) stenosis to the ratio of CMB (Number of CMB in MCA territory/Total counts of MB) separately. Associations of intracranial atherosclerosis severity with imaging and clinical markers of small vessel disease burden were analyzed. Results: Of 100 patients with probable CAA, 57 (57%) had MRAimaging. On multivariable regression analysis, total CMB number was associated with hypertension(p ¼ 0.06) and HbA1C level(p ¼ 0.035) and the presence of left MCA stenosis was inverselyrelatedtoCMBratioofleftMCAterritory(p ¼ 0.02).Patients with multifocal ICAS were associated with female sex (p ¼ 0.027) and Fazekas scale (p ¼ 0.023). Conclusions: Vascular risk factor exposure increase the risk of CMB in lobar region. The apparent paradoxical as-sociationofICASandCMBratiomayreﬂecteffectofhydrostaticpres- sure in CAA patients. Background: Recentworkhassuggestedthathyperphosphorylatedtau neuroﬁbrillary tangles underlie cognitive dysfunction in Alzheimer’s Disease (AD). Polygenic risk scores represent cumulative genome-wide genetic risk and may be useful in identifying individuals at risk of developing AD. We investigate associations between polygenic risk score for AD and regional brain tau deposition measured using 18 F-AV-1451-PET imaging in cognitively normal (CN), Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI), and AD individuals. Methods: Pre-processed 18 F-AV-1451-PET and MRI images were downloaded from the ADNI database. A partial volume correction (PVC) method was applied, as previously described (Tohka and Reilhac, 2008). After PET spatial normalization, regions of interest (ROI) were deﬁned in standard space and standardized uptake value ratio (SUVR) images werecomputed.PolygenicriskscorefordevelopingADwascalculatedforeachindividualaspreviouslydescribed(Desikanetal.,2017).Poly-genicriskscorewasrelatedtoeachROISUVRseparatelyforCN,MCIandADparticipantsusingageneralizedlinearmodel. Results: 103CN, 77MCIand24ADparticipantswereincludedandmatchedinsex,pro-portion polygenic risk healthy and MCI individuals. Notably, the association between polygenic risk and tau deposition is independent of cognitive status and amyloidogenesis. Background: Poor sleep quality is associated with structural brain changes and increased Alzheimer disease (AD) risk. Sleep quality has also been found to have an interaction with apoE-4 on AD risk (Lim et al, JAMA Neurol. 2013). Whether such an interaction is also present with cortical thickness in cognitively healthy adults is unknown. We therefore tested the hypothesis that there is an interaction of sleep quality and apoE genotype on the cortical thickness of AD-associated brain regions in cognitively healthy adults. Methods: Imaging, genetics, and health data for 6247 cognitively healthy adults with ages ranging from 45 to 80 years were obtained from the UK Biobank Resource. FreeSurfer-derived cortical thickness measurements were used to create an AD thickness signature consisting of the temporal, inferior parietal, and posterior cingulate regions. Self-reported sleep quality assessed the frequency of sleep difﬁculties, categorized by “never/rarely”, “sometimes”, and “usually”. A linear model adjusting for sex, age, education, alcohol consumption, smoking, diabetes, BMI, intracranial volume, and principal components estimating population substructure was used to test the interaction effect of apoE-4 and sleep quality. Post-hoc analysis of subjects stratiﬁed by sleep quality was used to further investigate this interaction. Results: We foundasigniﬁcantinteractioneffectofsubjectivesleepqualityandapoEgenotypeontheADcorticalthicknesssignature.Thelikelihoodratiotestshowedthatthemodelincludingthisinteractiontermﬁtthedatasignif-icantly better than one without ( c 2 (2) ¼ 8.58, p ¼ 0.014). After adjusting for covariates, carrying apoE-4 was estimated to be associated with a 0.0134 mm increase in thickness among those without sleep difﬁculties (p ¼ 0.0158), a 0.00096 mm decreaseamong thosewith occasional sleep difﬁculties (p ¼ 0.799), and a 0.0091 mm decrease among those with usual sleep difﬁculties (p ¼ 0.055). Conclusions: In this cross-sectional study of cognitively healthy adults, we found a signiﬁcant interaction ofapoE-4andsubjectivesleepqualityontheADcorticalthicknesssigna-ture.Amongthosewithfrequentsleepdifﬁculties,apoE-4carriershadadecreasedthicknessrelativetononcarriers.Amongthosewithoutre-ported sleep difﬁculties, apoE-4 carriers had an increased thickness rela-tive to non-carriers. Background: There is an established association of adiposity (obesity) with higher dementia risk and faster cognitive decline, and with their associated brain damage.
